speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four, interviewing William McKinney. Mr. McKinney, is there anything else that you would like to mention or that we have not discussed in previous tape, particularly related to the Camp Buchenwald that you have seen, that you may not have mentioned-- mentioned in the previous tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I saw most of the atrocities.

speaker: interviewer
text: Most of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: War-related atrocities that were committed by the SS. And the German army, the Wehrmacht, they call it. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of other things have you seen? Have you met any of the Germans in a camp, in Buchenwald itself? Have you had any encounter with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The soldiers?

speaker: interviewer
text: Any Germans in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I met and talked with Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have any response to what happened at Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- many of them just seemed to turn a deaf ear to it. They didn't want to hear about it, the civilians that I met over there. And many of them were aware of it. And many of them were not aware of what was going on in those concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: You spoke of seeing a lamp shade made out of human skin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you have seen that kind of caught your attention?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They had brains pickled in formaldehyde, hearts pickled in formaldehyde. Preserved.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have seen that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this? Where did you see it?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Camp Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it in some kind of a laboratory? Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a laboratory. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, when were you discharged?

speaker: interviewee
text: December 17, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- when did you-- but before discharge, were you discharged in the States, or were you still in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you get back to-- to the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: By December 15, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were discharged--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Indiantown Gap, Pennsylvania.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like to-- to return home to-- to the States after the war and after seeing concentration camp atrocities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, whenever we arrived in the States, they had a big dinner prepared for us at-- at Indiantown Gap, a steak dinner. Potatoes and vegetables and so forth. But there was something that my body was crying for. It wasn't no meat and potatoes. Milk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Milk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And this mess sergeant told me that most of the soldiers coming back was craving this milk, fresh milk, because we had to use powdered milk the whole time we were over there. And I took a number 56 dipper, which is about a quart, and I stood there. They had a big 20-gallon pot with a spigot on it, merged in ice to keep it nice and cold. And I take this number 56 dipper, which is about a quart, and I drank about four of those. And they had-- was no room for no steak and potatoes. [LAUGHTER] I just wanted milk. And when I got home, my mother was getting two quarts of milk a day for the family. I told her, put two more. I'll pay for it. Get two more quarts. So she ordered two more quarts. Then as I settled down, I would get in the mornings when I'd get up, I'd eat a half a pound of bacon, about six eggs, and drink a quart of milk for breakfast [INAUDIBLE] before I had eggs and bacon, mostly. And then cereals, oatmeal, and all that sort of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came home, did you get married? You got married, I understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see. I got home in '45. Three years later I got married. I got married in '48. October 6, 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have three children, three boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a little bit about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my oldest son--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: William Paul, Jr. He has a degree in architecture. He graduated University of Pennsylvania. Got his degree in architecture. Then he got his master's in ecology from Slippery Rock University. My second son works for a board of education here in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he a teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a teacher. He just got his certification-- oh-- I guess it was last year or-- he's a certified teacher now. Before he was sub teaching.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your third son? What is your name of your second son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Christopher.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your third son?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's deceased. His name was Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had muscular dystrophy.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he passed away?

speaker: interviewee
text: About four years now. Seeing this Dr. Danowski, who was in charge of research. He was under his direct observation. And Dr. Danowski had told me the type of muscular dystrophy that he had. A life expectancy of people with that type was between 35 and 40. He lived to be 38. He had his degree in business administration. He was a tax accountant. He worked for Internal Revenue downtown. He worked for the city in their tax division. And then, when he was working for Internal Revenue, they-- most of the other personnel working there, they would load his desk up with all this work, paperwork, whilst they goofed off at the water fountain and the coffee machine. And I told him, no, you don't need that. And so I made him give it up, because anytime you have a disability, you automatically get $150 Social Security. And so I didn't think that was adequate for a young man, so I put him on my Social Security. And so they took so much out of my Social Security and added to it and made him get about $300 of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Mr. McKinney, it's 52 years after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you would meet your two little friends who were four and six today, what it be like? What would you say to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd be delighted to meet them. I'd be-- I'd be delighted to meet them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you say to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you know, I would say how wonderful, fine young men you've grown up to be and so forth. And just be overwhelmed to see that they had prospered. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think they would say to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I have no idea. They would probably say thank you for feeding us [DRINKS] when we were hungry. But I doubt very seriously. I don't know where they are today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you've been fighting in the war. You entered the Camp Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think it is important to give testimony of what you have seen during the war, and particularly of the atrocities as you have said that you have seen in Buchenwald? Why is it important?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why is it important? Well, it's important to let the generation come-- coming along know that life is not all peaches and cream. And some-- and to be a true red-blooded American, sometime you have to make certain sacrifices. And making sacrifices is not fighting among each other and getting involved with the wrong people.

speaker: interviewer
text: I never dreamed that I'd seen drugs and so forth destroying our communities and so forth. I never dreamed that I'd see gangsters and racketeers taking over, getting the upper hand in our communities and so forth. And even today, I-- I never thought that I would see the day come where every time you turn the television on, it's full with nothing but violence. Yeah. So what's the society coming to? Is there anything else you would like to say that we may not have-- that you may not have spoken about before during your testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would be-- before your final message, is there anything that you would like to say what you did after you were liberated, besides having family? Did you-- what kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I mean after-- I mean, freed from service.

speaker: interviewee
text: After I had completed my tour of duty in the military, well, after the war in Europe, I was in the Army Reserve. And came out of the Army Reserve and went back and served an additional year, assisting the wounded soldiers at Howard General Hospital on Staten Island returning home. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's where I made sergeant.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you were discharged, you were discharged with the rank of sergeant?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was discharged with the rank of PFC. At Howard General Hospital, where I was promoted to sergeant, then I was-- then I came out, got discharged from-- I was getting ready to make a career out of the Army. I got discharged because of family concerns. I went to work. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was up to be promoted to master sergeant and shipped back over to Germany because of my previous experience in Germany. But I turned it down. When I was discharged a second time, I turned it down, come home to assist with securing my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would be your message to the young people, to the world, before we end your testimony? What would you say to them? To your children and your grandchildren-- your grandson?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's time to lend every effort of one's being in trying to establish peace and harmony throughout communities, throughout these United States of America, and throughout the world without bloodshed. It's time that we come to our senses and eliminate bloodshed. Because war is hell. War is horrible. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.
